Trending Tensions

A simple model of attention, ideology, and polarization online

A simple model of attention, ideology, and polarization online

by Leon Olszewski, Kateryna Maikovska, and Annie Waldherr

May 2026

This explorable represents an online discussion space where issues compete for the attention of social media users. With a simple attention mechanism, the model simulates how certain topics can capture widespread interest over extended periods of time, while others struggle to gain traction.

The explorable also incorporates political ideologies, placing users and topics along a spectrum of political leanings. This feature lets us observe how political alignment influences attention dynamics and shapes the rise and fall of issues in the online space. The model provides insight into the mechanisms behind viral hashtag campaigns, such as #MeToo or #BlackLivesMatter, which once dominated online discussions and mobilized substantial grass-roots support, known as connective action.

This is how it works

The position of both entities—users (the little dots) and topics (the speech bubbles)—encodes two meanings:

  • The horizontal axis represents a one-dimensional political spectrum. You can think of it as the "left vs. right wing" debate in politics. For users, this signifies their political leanings, while for topics their media frames.
  • The vertical axis is divided into equally spaced sections which are always occupied by a discussion topic of a certain color. The topics move higher up the more attention they get and fall down when the interest fades. The color carries no meaning except that it visually differentiates the topics. You can think of topics as news stories, hashtag campaigns, debates on social issues, or other forms of online discussion.

Each user has a fixed political leaning value, represented by their position on the horizontal axis, and follows one topic at a time, indicated by their switching color. Users move towards their current topic along the vertical axis and once there, they slightly jiggle which represents a heated discussion around the target issue.

Attention mechanism

Users’ attention is modeled through a threshold-based switching mechanism. Users select and maintain their current topics based on four aspects:

  1. Ideological alignment: Users prefer topics which are ideologically close to them. This translates to the distance between a user and a topic on the horizontal axis which corresponds to their political leanings.
  2. Popularity: Humans are social animals who prefer things that their peers like. A topic's popularity is measured by the share of the users which are currently paying attention to it.
  3. Topic's newsworthiness: Topics have an attribute that models their inherent social relevance — think about the difference between a cat picture trending versus a local hurricane warning. Newsworthiness is randomly drawn from an exponential distribution with λ = 1.5. Topics’ newsworthiness is represented visually via their relative size: the bigger the speech bubble, the higher the newsworthiness.
  4. Topic's age: Topics have a maximum age that depends on their popularity. The older a topic is, the less attractive it is. Eventually, topics die and new ones will appear. Topics’ aging is represented visually by topics’ colors fading.

At each step, each user’s satisfaction with their current topic is computed as the weighted sum of the first three aspects minus the topic’s age. If the computed index is below a threshold of 0.4, the user will switch to a new random topic.

Observe this

When you run the simulation with the initial parameters, one or two topics will gather most user’s attention after a certain amount of time — usually those are topics with a relatively high newsworthiness value. Follow the pie chart to observe the attention distribution. This is measured by a normalized Gini Index, which rises towards 1 when the attention is unequally distributed.

Then, turn up the "Importance of ideology" slider. You will observe that the users have significantly more trouble deciding on a single important topic. Moreover, less newsworthy topics have an increased chance to become the star of the show.

Try simulating a non-polarized society by clicking the toggle on the right. Now, the users’ ideology is normally distributed, with more people in the middle of the spectrum and less on the fringes. Observe how now mostly topics in the political center tend to dominate. A relevant explanation of how opinion distributions arise can be found in the echo chambers explorable.

Notice how...

To dominate the attention landscape, a topic needs to be not only newsworthy and ideologically appealing to many users, but also to appear in the right moment of time. For instance, think about the enormously impactful case of the #MeToo movement which caught attention globally in 2017. The now established movement, however, has existed in the fringes of media attention since 2006 as it was founded as a local youth program and a web page by Taran Burke.

Thus, the Trending Tensions explorable illustrates the difficulty of predicting the next hot topic in a volatile media environment. The model parameters represent multiple factors at play and the simulation lets us explore their interaction effects.

Try comparing the model to your familiar environment: Think about what ideology distribution would describe your community best? How do you perceive the importance of ideology in the news that you see online? And what are the model settings that best resemble what you observe on social media? Lastly, the most intriguing question: How can we predict when the next #MeToo rises?

Acknowledgements

This interactive model is based on the complexity explorables template which is also used in many other interactive visualizations of complex phenomena. The site as well as the template are maintained by Dirk Brockmann from TU Dresden.

The Trending Tensions explorable is a Java Script implementation designed and programmed by Leon Olszewski. It is based on a NetLogo Social Issue Emergence conceptual agent-based model designed and programmed by Kateryna Maikovska and supervised by Professor Annie Waldherr, researchers at the Department of Communication, University of Vienna.

References

Bennett, W. L., & Segerberg, A. (2012). The logic of connective action: Digital media and the personalization of contentious politics. Information, Communication and Society, 15(5), 739-768.

Harcup, T., & O'neill, D. (2001). What is news? Galtung and Ruge revisited. Journalism studies, 2(2), 261-280. https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/14616700118449

Monzer, C. (2024). Aligning and deviating voices: Toward a theory of cultural resonance in political communication. [Doctoral dissertation, Norwegian University of Science and Technology].
